Facts.text: Arab forces under Amr ibn al-'As conquer Alexandria
Battle of Kapetron: night battle between invading Seljuk Turks and Byzantine-Georgian forces of Constantine IX, Byzantines drive back Turks but unable to stop huge plunder
At the Battle of Myriokephalon the Byzantines fail to recover Anatolia from Turkish rule
Jews are expelled from France by order of Charles VI
Battle of Świecino [Battle of Żarnowiec]: Kingdom of Poland decisively defeats a 2,700-man army of the Teutonic Knights near Krokowa, Poland during the Thirteen Years' War
Gent surrenders to Duke of Parma
Dutch sailors claim island of Mauritius for Netherlands - name it after Maurice, Prince of Orange and Count of Nassau
Dutch ship Sardam arrives to rescue those shipwrecked on the Batavia, off coast of western Australia, discovering a group led by Jeronimus Cornelisz had murdered 124 of the survivors
Battle of Breitenfeld: King Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden defeats Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly
French troops occupy Mainz
France & Spain sign Treaties of Nijmegen
Massachusetts Bay Colony granted new charter
Turkish coup under Mahmud I; Sultan Ahmed III flees
Georg-August University opens in Göttingen
Fortress Presidio of San Francisco is founded in New Spain to gain a foothold in Alta California and the San Francisco Bay
First treaty between the US and Indian tribes is signed at Fort Pitt
Prussian troops conquer Gorinchem
Treaty of Fredrikshamn is signed between Sweden and Russia, ending the Finnish War and ceding the territory of Finland to Russia
First whaling ship arrives in Hawaii
Fourth great fire in San Francisco
First class for escaped slaves taught by Mary Peake at Fortress Monroe Virginia (now Hampton University).
American Civil War: The Allegheny Arsenal explosion results in the single largest civilian disaster during the war; 78 workers killed.
Battle of Antietam [Battle of Sharpsburg]: Bloodiest day in the American Civil War with 22,000 dead, wounded, or missing in the first major battle on Union soil
Battle of Cumberland Gap, Tennessee - evacuated by Federals
Battle of Mumfordville, Kentucky, US Col J Wilder surrenders city
Pope Pius IX encyclical On Persecution published in New Grenada
American Civil War: Grant approves Sheridan's plan for Shenandoah Valley Campaign
Mont Cenis railway tunnel in Switzerland opens
Phillip W. Pratt patents his sprinkler system for extinguishing fires
19 students attend opening class at Ohio State University
Race riots in South Carolina
1st British troops leave Bombay for South Africa
Philippine–American War: Filipinos under Juan Cailles defeat Americans under Colonel Benjamin F. Cheatham at Mabitac.
The Commonwealth of Australia is proclaimed
USA, Anthracite coal miners go out on strike for better wages until the October 25, by which time the owners are persuaded that their stand is harming President McKinley's campaign
US protests anti-semitism in Romania
Boston Pilgrims clinch AL pennant, beating Cleveland, 14-3
Thomas Selfridge becomes first fatality of powered flight
Denis Peyrony and Louis Capitan discover the skull of an adult male Neanderthal (La Ferrassie 1) during excavations in a rock shelter near La Ferrassie, France
1st transcontinental airplane flight, NY-Pasadena in 82 hrs 4 min
Center fielderCasey Stengelbreaks in with Brooklyn & hits 4 singles
40,000 Amsterdam demonstrators demand general voting right
Cards set a record of 12 consecutive hits in 4th (10) & 5th (2) innings
National Football League is born in Canton, Ohio; 12 teams pay $100 each to join American Professional Football Association; renamed the NFL in 1922
Radio Moscow begins transmitting (12 KWs-most powerful station)
Sutton Vane's "Outward Bound," premieres in London
Italy signs treaty of Rapallo
Hurricane hits Miami & Palm Beach Florida; about 450 die
Boston Braves pitcher Ray Boggs hits 3 batters in 1 inning during his 4th and final major league appearance, in 15-5 loss to Chicago Cubs
San Felipe Segundo/Okeechobee hurricane makes landfall in West Palm Beach-Lake Okeechobee, Florida as category 4 storm, killing 2,500- 3,000 and causing $25M in damages (about $345M in 2020 dollars)
British troops begin withdrawal from occupied Germany
1st LP record demonstrated (RCA Victor, NYC), venture failed
Boston Red Sox outfielder Earl Webb sets record with 65 en route to 67 MLB doubles
Operetta "Viktoria & Her Hussar" by Paul Abraham (adapted for English by Harry Graham) premieres in London at Palace Theatre
USSR joins League of Nations (Netherland, Switzerland & Portugal vote no)
1st NFL game in Washington, D.C.; Redskins beat NY Giants 13-3
German U-29 sinks British aircraft carrier Courageous, 519 die
Poland's President Ignacy Mościck and Prime Minister Felicjan Sławoj Składkowski flee to Romania
Soviet Union invades Eastern Poland allowing Germans to advance West, taking 217,000 Poles prisoner, without a formal declaration of war
Taisto Mäki of Finland becomes first man to run 10,000 metres in under 30 minutes, in a time of 29:52.6 in Helsinki
Nazis deprive Jews of possessions
The New Zealand Labour Party abolishes the death penalty (It is reintroduced by the National government in 1950 before being finally removed from the statute book in 1961)

World War II: A decree issued by the Soviet State Committee of Defense, restoring Vsevobuch "Universal compulsory military training of the citizens of the USSR" in the face of the "Great Patriotic War"
Load of "ammunition in transit" explodes at Norfolk Naval Air Station
World War II: Soviet city of Bryansk is liberated from Nazi control
Dutch begin railroad strike against German occupiers
Operation Market Garden: In the largest airborne operation of WWII, Allied paratroopers land in the Netherlands in a failed attempt to capture the Arnhem bridge over the Rhine
James Forrestal sworn in as 1st US Secretary of Defense
KCOP TV channel 13 in Los Angeles/Hollywood, CA (IND) begins
WLS TV channel 7 in Chicago, IL (ABC) begins broadcasting
128 die as fire guts Canadian passenger steamer Noronic in Toronto
3rd Cannes Film Festival: "The Third Man" directed by Carol Reed wins the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
North Atlantic Treaty Council meets for 1st time
WFAA TV channel 8 in Dallas-Fort Worth, TX (ABC) begins broadcasting
San Francisco 49ers (formerly AAFC) play 1st NFL game, lose 21-17
Romanian bishop A. Pacha of Timisoara sentenced to 18 years
"I am an American Day" & "Constitution Day" renamed "Citizenship Day"
Black students enter Clay elementary school in Kentucky
Television is first broadcast in Australia
Yanks clinch pennant #22 on Mantle's 50th homer of year
KETV TV channel 7 in Omaha, NB (ABC) begins broadcasting
Scott Crossfield takes X-15 up for 1st powered flight
Thailand military coup under marshal Sarit Thanarat
The North East Humanists group is founded in Newcastle upon Tyne
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Transit 1A, 1st navigational satellite launched; failed to orbit
Typhoon kills 2,000 in Japan & Korea
Cuba nationalizes US banks
"Car 54, Where are You?" premieres on US TV
Minnesota Vikings' 1st NFL game (beat Chicago Bears 37-13)
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Justice Department files 1st suit to end segregation in public schools
US space officials announce selection of 9 new astronauts
"The Fugitive" starring David Janssen premieres on ABC TV
Train struck makeshift bus full of migrant workers, killing 32
National Museum of Anthropology, the largest museum in Latin America, inaugurated by President Adolfo López Mateos in Mexico City

The Beatles are paid a then-record $150,000 by baseball team owner Charles Finley for a concert at Municipal Stadium in Kansas City, Missouri, on a scheduled day off; the group adds the song "Kansas City"/"Hey, Hey, Hey, Hey" to their standard setlist, much to the delight of the crowd
CBS premiere of WWII sitcom "Hogan's Heroes"
WPHL TV channel 17 in Philadelphia, PA (IND) begins broadcasting
"Mission Impossible" premieres on CBS-TV
Cleveland pitchers set AL record striking out 19 batters in 1st 9 innings
Mount Washington cog railway train derails, kills 8 (NH)
New Orleans Saints 1st NFL game, they lose to LA Rams 27-13
San Francisco GiantsGaylord Perryno-hits St Louis Cardinals, 1-0
Zond 5 completes circumnavigation of Moon
American TV variety program "The Flip Wilson Show" debuts on NBC-TV,; runs for 4 seasons, winning 2 Emmy Awards
Jordan launches offensive against guerrilla army
WSWP TV channel 9 in Grandview, WV (PBS) begins broadcasting
BART begins passenger service in San Francisco
Tanzania troops march in to Uganda
Courageous (US) beats Southern Cross (Aust) in 23rd America's Cup
Amnesty International receives Erasmus-prize
NASA publicly unveils the space shuttle Enterprise in Palmdale, California, named after the Star Trek Enterprise, with the cast attending
Rollout of 1st space shuttle orbiter Enterprise (OV-101)
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
30th Emmy Awards: "All in the Family"; Ed Asner & Sada Thompson win
After 6 losses to the New York Yankees in September, the Boston Red Sox finally score a 7-3 win over their arch rivals at Yankee Stadium
Pietro Mennea runs world record 200 m (19.72")
Russian Bolshoi Ballet dancers Leonid & Valentina Kozlov granted political asylum in US
Iraq breaks 1975 treaty with Iran and proclaims sovereignty over Shatt al-Arab waterway
Iraq under Saddam Hussein signs accord with Algeria
Oak A's Rick Langford is removed with 2 outs in 9th inning ending his consecutive complete-game streak at 22
Chicago White Sox clinch their 1st-ever AL West championship
Reggie Jacksonis 13th to hit 500 HRs
USSR performs underground nuclear test
Soyuz T-14 carries 3 cosmonauts to Salyut 7 space station
Bomb attack in Paris, 6 killed
Marina Stepanova of USSR sets 400m hurdle woman's record (52.94)
Mets clinch NL East Championship
US Senate confirms William Rehnquist as 16th chief justice
Philadelphia celebrates 200th anniversary of Constitution
24th Olympic games open at Seoul, Korea
Jeff Reardon becomes 1st to record 40 or more saves in both AL & NL
-21] Hurricane Hugo, kills 85 in Charleston, South Carolina
41st Emmy Awards: "LA Law", "Cheers", Dana Delany & Candice Bergen win
Hurricane Hugo begins 4 day sweep through Caribbean, killing 62
NYC court of appeals overturns lower court decision & returns America's Cup back to US (from NZ)
Newspaper Guild votes 242-35 to keep NY Post publishing
Soviet Union & Saudi Arabia restore diplomatic ties
4,355 turn out to see Expos play NY Mets at Shea Stadium
The first version of the Linux kernel (0.01) is released to the Internet
UN admits Estonia, Latvia, Lithuiania, North & South Korea, Marshall Islands & Micronesia
Amsterdam stock exchange hits record Ÿ8.8 billion
House votes 280 to 128 to give FCC control of cable TV rates
EmperorFrederick IBarbarossa issues the 'Privilegium Minus' decree, which elevated Austria to a duchy
Jews are to be expelled from France by order of KingCharles VI
Pope Clement VIII recognizesHenry IVas King of France
Dutch scientistAntonie van Leeuwenhoekis the first to report the existence of bacteria
Edinburgh occupied by Jacobites underCharles Edward Stuart(aka the Young Pretender or Bonny Prince Charlie)
William Herscheldiscovers Mimas, satellite of Saturn
Charles Darwins lands on Chatham in Galapagos archipelago
Harriet Tubman1st escapes slavery in Maryland with two of her brothers
James Donnellyis sentenced to hang for murdering Patrick Farrell, but a petition for clemency reduces his sentence to 7 years in Kingston Penitentiary
Joshua Abraham Norton, an English-born resident of San Francisco, proclaims himself his Imperial MajestyEmperor NortonI, Emperor of the United States of America
Battle at Elands River Port: Boer GeneralJan Smutsdestroys British 17th Lancers unit
Battle of Blood River Port: Boer commandos led byLouis Bothadefeat a British Mounted Infantry force commanded by Major Hubert Gough
Playing as "Sullivan" Columbia University jrEddie Collinsdebuts with A's
Andrew Fisherbecomes Prime Minister of Australia for the third time
World War I flying ace,the Red Baronof the German Luftstreitkräfte, wins his first aerial combat near Cambrai, France
American baseball shortstopHonus Wagnerretires at 43, Pirates retire his #33
CyclistPiet Moeskopsbecomes world sprint champ
Charles Lindberghvisits San Francisco
RCA Victor releases 1st 33 1/3 rpm recording -Beethoven's Fifth Symphony performed by the Philadelphia Orchestra, led by Leopold Stokowski at the Philadelphia Academy of Music
Manuel L. Quezony Molina is elected the second President of the Philippines
British premierNeville Chamberlainleaves Munich
Adolf Hitlerindefinitely postpones Operation Sealion, the planned German invasion of Great Britain
Famous meeting between Danish physicistNiels Bohrand German head of nuclear energy projectWerner Heisenbergin Copenhagen to discuss nuclear weapons (date approximate)
Stan Musialmakes his major league debut for the St. Louis Cardinals, going 2-for-4
British PremierWinston Churchilltravels to US
Jackie Robinsonis named Rookie of Year by Sporting News
Ernie Banksbecomes Chicago Cubs 1st black player
"Lord of the Flies" byWilliam Goldingis published by Faber and Faber in London
In a quick re-match at Yankee Stadium, NYC,Rocky MarcianoKOsEzzard Charlesin the 8th round to retain his world heavyweight boxing title
Future MLB Hall of Famer Baltimore OrioleBrooks Robinsongoes 2-4 in his 1st game
Two male attorneys "stand in" as actressSophia Loren& producer Carlo Ponti wed by proxy in Juarez, Mexico

Fran Tarkentonplays his first NFL Game against the Chicago Bears, coming off the bench to lead the Vikings to a 37-13 victory, also becoming the only QB to throw four touchdown passes in his first career game
Samuel Beckett's "Happy Days," premieres in NYC
American sitcom "Bewitched", starringElizabeth Montgomery, premieres on ABC TV
Motown Records releasesThe Supremes' single "Baby Love"; written and produced by the Holland-Dozier-Holland team, it becomes their second consecutive #1 record
YankeeMickey Mantlegets career hits #1999, 2000 & 2001 & his 450th HR in 6-2 victory over Los Angeles Angels at New York
American rock band "The Doors" appear for 1st and last time on "TheEd SullivanShow"; singerJim Morrisonreneges on promise to change a lyric, prompting producers to offer no further invitations
British rock band "The Who" make US television debut, performing "My Generation" on "The Smothers Brothers Comedy Hour", and detonatingKeith Moon's drum kit

TV comedy series "M*A*S*H", adapted from the movie, starringAlan Alda,Loretta Swit, Wayne Rogers, and McLean Stevenson, debuts on CBS in the US and runs for 11 years, garnering 14 Emmy Awards and 1 Peabody
Stephen Sondheim's musical "A Little Night Music" transfers from the Shubert to the Majestic Theater, NYC
British punk rock bandthe Sex Pistolsplay before a captive audience at Chelmsford Prison, Essex, England
Polydor/Atlantic releasesRingo Starr' fifth studio album "Ringo's Rotogravure"
British-American bandFleetwood Mac's "Rumours" album is #1 for 19th straight week
Dave Kingmanhits his 1st Yankee homer, Reggie hits 2 more
Anwar Sadat,Menachem BeginandJimmy Cartersign theCamp David Accords, frameworks for peace in the Middle East and between Egypt and Israel
Royals'George Brettis 6th to have 20 doubles/triples/HRs in a season
Bette Midler's concert film "Divine Madness", directed by Michael Ritchie, premieres
Polish workers under the leadership ofLech Wałęsafound the Solidarity movement at the Gdańsk Shipyard
South Korea opposition leaderKim Dae-jungsentenced to death on false charges of sedition and conspiracy (sentence later commuted)
MLB Los Angeles Dodgers pitcherFernando Valenzuelasets NL rookie record with 8th shutout of season
"Bad to the Bone" single byGeorge Thorogoodand the Destroyers first released
Brian Mulroneysworn in as Canada's 18th Prime Minister, succeedingJohn Turner
Dwight Goodenties record of 32 strikeouts in consecutive games
PopeJohn Paul IIarrives in San Francisco, meets with AIDS patients, and embraces an AIDS infected child
Frank Zappaconducts The Ensemble Modern in portions of his classical work "The Yellow Shark" in Frankfurt, Germany; receives a 20 minute standing ovation in what is his last public appearance
US Constitution is signed by delegates at the Philadelphia Convention
Charles III, The Simple, King of West Francia (893-922), King of Lotharingia (911-23), granted Vikings the Duchy of Brittany, born in France (d. 929)
Minamoto no Sanetomo, 3rd Japanese shōgun of the Kamakura shogunate (1203-19) and waka poet, born in Japan (d. 1219)
Pope Paul V [Camillo Borghese], 233rd pope (1605-21) and persecutor of Galileo, born in Rome (d. 1621)
Charlotte Brabantia, Countess of Nassau and daughter of William I of Orange, born in Antwerp (d. 1631)
Francesco Sacrati, Italian composer, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1650)
John Lambert, English general-major and parliamentary leader, born in Kirkby Malham, England (d. 1684)
Ranuccio II Farnese, Duke of Parma, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (now Italy) (d. 1694)
Hans Herr, Swiss Mennonite bishop, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1725)
Gilbert Burnet, Scottish philosopher and Bishop of Salisbury, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1715)
Stephen Hales, English physiologist, chemist and inventor (first person to measure blood pressure), born in Bekesbourne, Kent, England (d. 1761)
Durastante Natalucci, Italian historian (specialized in history of Trevi, in Umbria), born in Picicche di Trevi, Italy (d. 1772)
Maria Luisa of Savoy, Queen consort of Spain to Philip V (1701-14), born in Royal Palace of Turin, Savoy (d. 1714)
Ignaz Jakob Holzbauer, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Archduchy of Austria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1783)
Gottlieb Rabener, German author (Vom Misbrauch of the Satire), born in Wachau, Germany (d. 1771)
John Rutledge, American 2nd Chief Justice of the United States, born in Charleston, South Carolina, British America (d. 1800)
Robert Wainwright, English composer and church organist, baptized in Manchester, England (d. 1782)
John Goodricke, English deaf astronomer (first to notice that some variable stars were periodic), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1786)
Henri-Montan Berton, French composer, chiefly of operas, born in Paris, France (d. 1844)
Johann August Apel, German jurist and writer, born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1816)
Giuseppe Caspar Mezzofanti, Italian cardinal, linguist and hyperpolyglot (understood 70 languages), born in Bologna, Italy (d. 1849)
Samuel Prout, British water color painter, born in Plymouth, England (d. 1852)
Saverio Mercadante, Italian composer, born in Altamura, Bari, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1870)
Antonio Benedetto Antonucci, Italian cardinal and diplomat, born in Subiaco, Italy (d. 1879)
Franklin Buchanan, American first superintendent of the U.S. Naval Academy at Annapolis, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1874)
Edward William Lane, British lexicographer and translator (One Thousand & One Nights), born in Hereford, England (d. 1876)
Stefano Ronchetti-Monteviti, Italian composer (Pergolese), and educator (Milan Conservatory, 1850-81), born in Asti, Piedmont, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1882)
Halfdan Kjerulf, Norwegian composer (Brudefaerden i Hardanger), born in Christiania, Norway (d. 1868)
Leon Foucault, French physicist whose pendulum proved that the Earth rotates, born in Paris (d. 1868)
Marthinus Wessels Pretorius, 1st president of the Republic South Africa, born in Graaff Reinet, Cape Colony (d. 1901)
Earl Van Dorn, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Claiborne County, Mississippi (d. 1863)
Émile Augier, French dramatist, born in Valence, Drôme, Kingdom of France (d. 1889)
Arthur Saint-Leon, French composer and dancer, born in Paris, France (d. 1870)
Lucius Quintus Cincinnatus Lamar, American politician and U.S. Supreme Court Justice, born in Eatonton, Georgia (d. 1893)
Berhard Riemann, German mathematician (zeta-function), born in Breselenz, Hanover (d. 1866)
Edouard Pailleron, French attorney, poet and stage writer, born in Paris, France (d. 1899)
Ira Erastus Davenport, American magician, claimed to be a spirit medium, born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1911)
Vaclav Juda Novotny, Czech composer and music critic, born in Vesce, Kingdom of Bohemia. Austrian Empire (d. 1922)
Clemens Baeumker, German historian (Patristischen Philosophie), born in Paderborn, Germany (d. 1924)
David Dunbar Buick, Scottish-American automobile pioneer (Buick Motor Company), born in Arbroath, Angus, Scotland (d. 1929)
Hans Müller, German music historian and writer, born in Cologne, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1897)
Konstantin Tsiolkovsky, Russian pioneer in rocket and space research, born in Izhevskoye, Ryazan Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1935)
Frank Dawson Adams, Canadian geologist, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1942)
Richard Henry Warren, American organist and composer, born in Albany, New York (d. 1933)
Owen Seaman, British poet and editor (Punch), born in Shrewsbury, England (d. 1936)
James Alexander Calder, Canadian politician, born in Oxford County, Ontario (d. 1956)
Christian Lous Lange, Norwegian pacifist and internationalist (Nobel Peace Prize 1921), born in Stavanger, Norway (d. 1938)
Ben Turpin, American comic (Uncle Tom Without the Cabin), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1940)
Vyacheslav Gavrilovich Karatigin, Russian composer, born in Pavlovsk, Russian Empire (d. 1925)

Vincenzo Tommasini, Italian composer (Le donne de buon umore), born in Rome, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1950)

Rube Foster, American Baseball HOF manager and executive (Negro National League pennant 1920–22, 26; Chicago American Giants), born in Calvert, Texas (d. 1930)
Désiré Inghelbrecht, French composer, conductor and writer, born in Paris (d. 1965)

Bastiaan de Gaay Fortman, Dutch historian and Justice of Curacao, born in Amsterdam (d. 1961)
Charles Tomlinson Griffes, American composer (White Peacock), born in Elmira, New York (d. 1920)

Uzeyir Hajibeyov, Soviet composer who composed the anthem used by Azerbaijan during the Soviet period, born in Agjabadi, Russian Empire (d. 1948)

Lubov Tchernicheva, Russian-British ballerina, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1976)
Hendrik Andriessen, Dutch organist and composer (Ballade of Merel; Te Deum), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1981)

James Anderson, Australian tennis player (Australasian C'ship 1922, 24, 25), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1973)

Mary Stewart, British sci-fi author (Crystal Cave), born in Sunderland, England (d. 2014)

Oswald Garrison Villard Jr., American Engineer (electronics) who invented the over-the-horizon radar that could detect objects thousands of miles away and peer around the Earth's curvature, born in Dobbs Ferry, New York (d. 2004)

Yumjaagiin Tsedenbal, Mongolian politician (Chairman of the Presidium of the People's Great Khural (President), 1974-84; Prime Minister, 1952-74; General Secretary of the Mongolian People's Party, 1940-54), born in Davst sum, Uvs aimag, Outer Mongolia (d. 1991)
